reasoning: The manager is explicitly contradicting written company policy regarding personal property, acting ultra vires. A reasonable person would immediately recognize that a supervisor does not have the authority to override official corporate IT policy to mandate invasive surveillance on a personal device.

A reasonable person would immediately agree that an employer has no jurisdiction over a back-office employee's legal, non-competitive personal hobbies conducted outside of work hours. The boss's attempt to enforce personal 'family values' on a private blog constitutes a clear overreach of the employment relationship boundaries.

No reasonable person would defend an employer's right to video record an employee's private home life 24/7, particularly during unpaid off-hours. While employers can monitor work, extending surveillance to sleeping and leisure time in a private residence is a clear overreach of jurisdictional authority that transcends the employment contract.

A manager demanding full medical diagnosis history for a single sick day—contradicting company policy which only requires a note for longer absences—is a clear overreach of authority. No reasonable person would defend a shift lead's right to access private medical codes or history under threat of termination for 'time theft'.

The enforcer is explicitly identified as a non-employee relative with no formal role, imposing a sexually discriminatory condition on access to essential work supplies. No reasonable person would recognize the authority of an owner's child who is not on the payroll to lock away business assets and demand female staff wear skirts to access them.
